title: Goodbye OpenVPN, and Hello Zerotier
summary: As a little gift for completing all 12(!) of my exams, my boyfriend bought me Stardew Valley . Because of DRM and whatnot, I got it through GOG instead of Steam. Stardew has a multiplayer feature, so my boyfriend set up a new multiplayer game, and sent me the invite code. I loaded up the thankfully Linux-native version of Stardew, clicked the multiplayer op…
content:
As a little gift for completing all 12(!) of my exams, my boyfriend bought me Stardew Valley. Because of DRM and whatnot, I got it through GOG instead of Steam. Stardew has a multiplayer feature, so my boyfriend set up a new multiplayer game, and sent me the invite code. I loaded up the thankfully Linux-native version of Stardew, clicked the multiplayer option, and… there's no place to put a multiplayer code in.

Hm.

So after some digging, Stardew's non-LAN multiplayer doesn't work without having GOG Galaxy running (or Steam, if I bought it through there). GOG Galaxy doesn't run on Linux, and I wasn't about to resign myself to running the game through Steam (regardless of how much I appreciate their Linux-native client).

The first thing I tried was running GOG Galaxy through Wine. After many many hours, that didn't work. I also tried Comet but it wasn't updated and it didn't seem to work with the latest Stardew (Comet didn't compile either, but that was easy enough to fix).

It has been four hours and I have yet to play Stardew.

So I ask myself, "Maybe I'm thinking about this the wrong way. There's gotta be another approach…" and lo and behold, the LAN functionality.

The reason I wanted to get the invite code working was because I didn't want my boyfriend to do anything on his computer to make everything work. I guess I had to remove that restriction, but I still want it to be as one-click as possible.

I also really really didn't wanna set up OpenVPN again. I didn't wanna bother with port-forwarding, nor firewall rules, nor anything else. I just wanted it to work. I just want the process for my boyfriend to look like this: Install an app, type in a code, press connect.

So no OpenVPN or Wireguard or anything else that requires me to open ports or do lots of configuration.

In another post, I looked into private network solutions. The first product that popped into my head was Tailscale, but that didn't seem like it could be self-hosted. The next image conjured out of spiritus mundi was Zerotier, which could be self-hosted. My boyfriend was getting frustrated, I was getting frustrated, so I did no more research than that. I looked at the self-hosting docs for Zerotier (which are a little lacking), and spun up a controller on my machine. I followed the instructions for creating a network and adding a client. It all seemed to work! With a little bit of apprehension, I had my boyfriend install the Zerotier desktop app, and put in the network code. I approved his computer, got the IP address of his machine, started up Stardew, and spent the next three hours furious at the fishing mechanics.

Yay! Problem solved blog post over.

But I would be loathe to stop there.

I did not like managing Zerotier from the CLI, especially with how unintuitive it was (cURL requests? Seriously?). I DuckDuckGo'd "zerotier gui" and found ZeroUI. It's still in active development, but it had almost every feature I needed. I put together a quick PR so I could run this behind mod_auth_openidc, and deployed the program.

Isn't that nice?

So, Zerotier is so much easier to self-host and deploy compared to OpenVPN or Tailscale. From the idea of using Zerotier to playing Stardew, it took me a grand total of 20 minutes. Modifying and deploying the UI was a little more difficult, but it was totally worth it.

I totally recommend checking out Zerotier. Stardew isn't a bandwidth-intensive program, nor is it latency-sensitive. Regardless, latency is all super low (sub 15 milliseconds) and a quick bandwidth test maxed out my WiFi connection. Maybe some tests are to come in the future, but I'm very happy with Zerotier. I set it up, and then my boyfriend and I forgot about it. I haven't had to wrangle firewall rules, nor have I seen any bizarre latency spikes, nor have I seen any other issues. It just works, and I appreciate that.
